epigraph.text:
Better a little with righteousness than much gain 
with injustice.

content.text:
In everyday life, we don't always need exact answers. When someone asks 
how far away a city is, we might say "about 200 miles" rather than 
"193.7 miles." When estimating a grocery bill, we round prices to make 
mental math easier. **Rounding** and **estimation** are practical 
skills that help us work with numbers efficiently.

**Rounding** replaces a number with a simpler number 
that is close in value. We round to a specified place value.

content.title: Rounding Whole Numbers
content.content:
To round a number to a given place:

1. Locate the digit in the place you are rounding to.

2. Look at the digit immediately to its right (the "test digit").

3. If the test digit is 5 or greater, round up (increase the 
 rounding digit by 1).

4. If the test digit is less than 5, round down (keep the rounding 
 digit the same).

5. Replace all digits to the right of the rounding place with zeros.

content.title: Rounding to the nearest ten
content.solution:
- The tens digit is 4 (in 8**4**7).

- The test digit (ones place) is 7.

- Since $7 \geq 5$, we round up: $4 \to 5$.

- Replace the ones digit with 0.
847 rounded to the nearest ten is **850**.

content.title: Rounding to the nearest hundred
content.solution:
- The hundreds digit is 3 (in 4,**3**28).

- The test digit (tens place) is 2.

- Since $2 < 5$, we round down: keep the 3.

- Replace the tens and ones with zeros.
4,328 rounded to the nearest hundred is **4,300**.

content.title: Rounding to the nearest thousand
content.solution:
- The thousands digit is 6 (in 7**6**,500).

- The test digit (hundreds place) is 5.

- Since $5 \geq 5$, we round up: $6 \to 7$.

- Replace digits to the right with zeros.
76,500 rounded to the nearest thousand is **77,000**.

content.title: Rounding with 9s
content.solution:
- The tens digit is 9 (in 9**9**7).

- The test digit is 7, so we round up.

- $9 + 1 = 10$, so we write 0 and carry 1 to the hundreds place.

- $9 + 1 = 10$, so we write 0 and carry 1 to the thousands place.
997 rounded to the nearest ten is **1,000**.

content.text:
**Estimation** uses rounding to quickly find an 
approximate answer. This is useful for:

items: Checking if a calculated answer is reasonable
Solving problems when exact answers aren't needed

content.title: Estimating a sum
content.solution:
Round each number to the nearest thousand:

- $3{,}847 \approx 4{,}000$

- $5{,}216 \approx 5{,}000$
Estimated sum: $4{,}000 + 5{,}000 = 9{,}000$

(The exact answer is 9,063, so our estimate is reasonable.)

content.title: Estimating a product
content.solution:
Round each number to the nearest ten:

- $48 \approx 50$

- $52 \approx 50$
Estimated product: $50 \times 50 = 2{,}500$

(The exact answer is 2,496, so our estimate is excellent!)

content.title: Estimating a quotient
content.solution:
Round to make the division easier:

- $8{,}742 \approx 8{,}000$ (or 9,000)

- $43 \approx 40$
Estimated quotient: $8{,}000 \div 40 = 200$

(The exact answer is about 203.3, so our estimate is close.)

content.text: Another estimation strategy uses only the front (leftmost) digits.

content.title: Front-end estimation
content.solution:
Use only the hundreds digits:
\[
300 + 500 + 200 = 1{,}000
\]
For a better estimate, look at the tens digits too: $40 + 80 + 10 = 130$

Adjusted estimate: $1{,}000 + 130 = 1{,}130$

(The exact answer is 1,148.)

content.text:
**Compatible numbers** are numbers that 
are easy to compute mentally. When estimating, we sometimes adjust 
numbers to make them compatible.

content.title: Using compatible numbers for division
content.solution:
Instead of rounding 2,347 to 2,000 (which doesn't divide evenly by 8), 
choose a compatible number:
\[
2{,}400 \div 8 = 300
\]
(The exact answer is about 293.4.)

content.title: Choosing compatible numbers
content.solution:
Think: What number close to 538 is divisible by 9?
\[
540 \div 9 = 60
\]
(The exact answer is about 59.8.)

content.text:
Estimation is valuable for catching errors. If your calculated answer 
is far from your estimate, you should check your work.

content.title: Checking with estimation
content.problem: A student calculated $347 \times 52 = 1{,}834$. Is this reasonable?
content.solution:
Estimate: $350 \times 50 = 17{,}500$

The calculated answer (1,834) is far too small. The student made an 
error. (The correct answer is 18,044.)

marginNotes.text:
Be sure you know the condition of your 
flocks, give careful attention to your herds.

The simple believe anything, but the 
prudent give thought to their steps.

marginNotes.author: Enrico Fermi
marginNotes.text:
Never make a calculation until you know the 
answer.
